figures.caption: Table 1: Comparison of loss functions for multivariate long-term forecasting. Prediction horizons H ∈ {96, 192, 336, 720}. The proposed APAL is highlighted in bold. Best prediction results are bolded; second-best are underlined.
Table 2: MSE vs APAL loss comparison (TSMixer) on H ∈ {96, 192, 336, 720} for public benchmark datasets. Best results in bold.
Table 3: Melbourne pedestrian dataset statistics (2010–2017) for the 16 sensors included in the released complete-coverage subset.
Table 4: Sensor locations for the 16 sensors in the Melbourne subset (WGS84).
Table 5: Summary of Datasets Used in Experiments

Table 8: Peak-structure diagnostic for APAL across the ten datasets. Class is assigned from S99, Rpeak, and Ftail using fixed thresholds. ∆MSE10 and ∆MSE1 APAL-vs.-MSE comparisons averaged over horizons trained on TSMixer backbone

Table 9: Comparison of loss functions for multivariate long-term forecasting (MAE metrics). Prediction horizons H ∈ {96, 192, 336, 720}. The proposed APAL is highlighted in bold. Best prediction results are bolded; second-best are underlined.
Table 10: Comparison of loss functions for multivariate long-term forecasting (Peak metrics). Prediction horizons H ∈ {96, 192, 336, 720}. Best results are bolded; second-best are underlined.

Figure 4: Marginal effects of APAL hyperparameters on MSE, MSE1, and MSE10 (Pedestrian Dataset, aggregated across horizons). Columns: (a) effect of λu, (b) effect of λp, (c) effect of τ . Lines in (a) and (b) represent different peak thresholds τ ∈ {0.8, 0.9, 0.95}.

Table 11: APAL hyperparameter ablation on ETT, Exchange, and Weather dataset with TSMixer architecture. Neutral denotes (λu, λp) = (1, 1), where APAL reduces to MAE. Gain and aggregateMSE change are relative to the neutral APAL setting for the same dataset and horizon. Best Peak F1 reports the maximum Peak F1 observed in the same grid.

Figure 5: Ablation study of APAL hyperparameters on the Pedestrian dataset, evaluated using MSE1 (mean squared error on the top 1% peak values). Each panel shows a heatmap of performance across the underestimation penalty, λu (rows), and the peak weight,λp (columns). Rows correspond to peak threshold values τ ∈ {0.80, 0.90, 0.95}, and columns correspond to forecast horizons H ∈ {96, 192, 336, 720}. Lower values (darker) indicate better peak prediction accuracy. Blue boxes highlight optimal configurations within each panel.

Figure 6: Qualitative comparison of long-horizon forecasts trained with MSE versus APAL loss. APAL reduces underestimation at high-demand periods in both the (a) Pedestrian and (b) Beach datasets. Values shown are standardized (z-score normalized).
Table 12: Comparison of Pinball Loss (q ∈ {0.9, 0.95}) and APAL Loss. Lower values are better. Best results are bold, second best are underlined. Model used shown in parentheses.
Table 13: Computational overhead of various loss functions compared to MSE loss. Per-epoch overhead measures the additional computation time per training epoch (in seconds).
